name: Network Address Translation (NAT)
description: Quiz on the operation, advantages, implementation and controversies of the NAT protocol.

questions.question: What does the NAT (Network Address Translation) protocol essentially do in a network?
options: It allows several local hosts to share a single public IP address on the way out.
Edge routers drop all malicious packets using IP rules.
It replaces physical copper cables with exclusive logical connections in local networks.
It encrypts the payload of each packet to provide local confidentiality.

questions.question: How does NAT distinguish the various local-host flows if they use the exact same public IP on the external network?
options: It manipulates and records the active logical port numbers at the transport layer.
It inserts a strict digital signature that identifies the packets from that router.
It requires the sender to always use the same route of cables and interfaces.
It splits the size of the internal bytes to avoid congestion on this side.

questions.question: Which of these options does NOT represent a 'private' IP address space commonly used inside local networks with NAT?
options: The range of addresses strictly starting with the standard global prefix 200.23.16.0/20.
The IP address space reserved and classified with the classic local prefix 10/8.
The block of private network addresses widely known as 172.16/12.
The range of standardized internal addresses starting with the common mask 192.168/16.

questions.question: What is the main cybersecurity advantage incidentally provided by the use of the NAT protocol?
options: Local hosts remain completely hidden and cannot be addressed from the outside.
The system can perfectly neutralize losses at the physical layer of the links.
The router blocks malicious timers that affect local flow control.
The network eliminates the continuous request for passwords during a secure session handshake.

questions.question: Why is the use of the NAT protocol often considered controversial from a purist network architecture point of view?
options: Routers should not change layer-four fields such as the port number.
Routers should use IPv6 exclusively instead of doing translation in hardware.
Protocols fail to send analog signals through the internal coaxial cables.
Web servers block packet switching if there is a failure when reading the bits.

questions.question: What specifically characterizes the problem known as NAT Traversal (NAT-T) in today's networks?
options: The difficulty of establishing connections when an external client tries to reach the NAT.
The extremely high propagation delay that occurs during circuit switching on the network.
The mandatory excessive fragmentation of data in the ISP's core routers.
The native inability of servers to resolve the domain name on the DNS server.

questions.question: To ensure bidirectional data communication, what does the edge router store in its 'NAT translation table'?
options: The pairing between the old source IP and port with the NAT's IP and its new port.
The complete history of the cryptographic keys generated in the client's handshake.
The continuous record of packets dropped by the gateway subnet's routers.
The exact physical control address of the computers' and phones' Wi-Fi interface.

questions.question: When a response packet returns from the Internet to your home network, what does the NAT router do with that datagram?
options: The router replaces the NAT IP with the original private IP by consulting its table.
The system blocks the message, requiring the host to initiate local contact again.
The router delegates the processing to the DNS to map the host correctly.
The protocol drops the datagrams to prioritize the active sends of that moment.

questions.question: As a network administrator, what is the great architectural advantage of adopting NAT translation internally in your organization?
options: Being able to change the devices' IPs on the network without having to notify the outside world.
Avoiding congestion failures by converting to circuit switching.
Replacing the need to use port numbers within the transport layer.
Isolating all data processing in a cache memory of cloud servers.

questions.question: Despite its purist limitations and the old hopes that IPv6 would eliminate it, what is the current state of NAT?
options: It remains fundamental and widely used in home networks and 4G and 5G mobile networks.
It was entirely banned by the IETF after the discovery of its security flaws.
It ended up being completely replaced as soon as the DNS servers were updated.
It operates exclusively inside global backbones with no access to real computers.
